tasks.title: Someone You Love Is in Crisis
tasks.what: What to do when you first realize someone you love has a substance use problem — or when a medical emergency forces the issue.
tasks.why: The first 24-72 hours after discovering or confronting addiction are critical. What you do — and what you avoid doing — sets the tone for everything that follows.
steps: Do not confront them while they are intoxicated or high. Wait until they are sober enough to have a conversation.
If they are in a medical emergency, call 911 or go to the ER. Their safety comes first.
If they are hospitalized, ask to speak with the hospital social worker or discharge planner about substance use treatment options BEFORE they are released.
Call the SAMHSA National Helpline: 1-800-662-4357. Free, confidential, 24/7. They can talk you through your options.
Write down what you have observed — dates, behaviors, incidents. This is for you, not to use against them.
Tell one trusted person in your life what is happening. You should not carry this alone.
Do not pour out their alcohol or flush their drugs. This can trigger dangerous withdrawal and escalate conflict.
Secure your own valuables, medications, and important documents.

terms.def: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration. Federal agency providing a free 24/7 helpline (1-800-662-4357) and treatment referrals.
Physical and psychological symptoms when someone stops using a substance. Can be life-threatening for alcohol and benzodiazepines.
Severe alcohol withdrawal involving confusion, rapid heartbeat, fever, and seizures. Medical emergency — can be fatal without treatment.

tasks.contacts: SAMHSA Helpline: 1-800-662-4357 (free, 24/7) • 911 for medical emergencies • Hospital social worker or discharge planner • Crisis Text Line: text HOME to 741741
mistakes: Googling "how to fix an addict" instead of calling SAMHSA — the internet is full of predatory treatment marketers. The helpline is free and unbiased.
Flushing pills or pouring out alcohol without medical guidance — abrupt withdrawal from alcohol, benzodiazepines, or opioids can cause seizures or death.
Making big decisions (kicking them out, filing for divorce, calling their employer) in the first 24 hours — give yourself time to think clearly before acting.
Assuming this is a one-time crisis — addiction is chronic. The actions you take now should be sustainable for the long haul, not just tonight.

tasks.title: What Addiction Is and How It Works
tasks.what: Understanding that addiction is a chronic brain disorder — not a moral failing — and how it changes behavior, decision-making, and relationships.
tasks.why: You cannot effectively support someone through addiction if you do not understand what it is. Education reduces blame, increases empathy, and helps you make informed decisions.
steps: Understand the brain science: addiction changes the brain's reward system, making the substance feel necessary for survival. This is why willpower alone is rarely enough.
Learn the difference between physical dependence and addiction. Dependence means the body needs the substance to function. Addiction includes compulsive use despite harmful consequences.
Recognize that Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) and Substance Use Disorder (SUD) exist on a spectrum — mild, moderate, and severe. Not everyone needs the same level of treatment.
Understand the role of co-occurring conditions. Over 50% of people with addiction also have depression, anxiety, PTSD, or another mental health condition.
Learn about the genetic component. Addiction has a 40-60% heritability rate — family history is a significant risk factor.
Research the specific substance involved. Different substances have different risks, withdrawal profiles, and treatment approaches.

terms.def: Alcohol Use Disorder. The clinical term for alcoholism, diagnosed on a spectrum: mild, moderate, severe.
Substance Use Disorder. The clinical umbrella term for addiction to any substance.
When someone has both a substance use disorder and a mental health condition. Both must be treated simultaneously.
Needing more of a substance to achieve the same effect. One of the early signs of dependence.

tasks.contacts: NIDA (National Institute on Drug Abuse): nida.nih.gov • SAMHSA: samhsa.gov • Al-Anon: al-anon.org
mistakes: Using shame-based language like "junkie" or "alcoholic" — clinical terms like "substance use disorder" reflect the medical reality and reduce stigma.
Researching only their substance and ignoring underlying conditions — over 50% of people with addiction also have untreated depression, anxiety, or PTSD.
Comparing their addiction to someone else's — "your uncle quit cold turkey" is not helpful. Each person's brain chemistry, substance, and circumstances are different.
Stopping your own education after one article — understanding addiction is an ongoing process. NIDA, SAMHSA, and Al-Anon all have free ongoing resources.

tasks.title: Recognizing the Warning Signs
tasks.what: How to identify behavioral, physical, and financial warning signs that someone you love may be struggling with substance use.
tasks.why: Early recognition leads to earlier intervention, and earlier intervention leads to better outcomes. Knowing what to look for helps you trust what you are seeing.
steps: Watch for behavioral changes: increased secrecy, defensiveness about drinking or drug use, withdrawing from family and friends, missing work or obligations.
Note physical signs: bloodshot eyes, unexplained weight changes, trembling hands, poor hygiene, frequent illness, sleep pattern changes.
Track financial red flags: unexplained withdrawals, missing money, unpaid bills, borrowing money frequently, selling possessions.
Observe relationship patterns: increased conflict, lying, breaking promises, blaming others, emotional volatility.
Document what you see in a private journal with dates and specific observations. This is not about building a case — it is about having clarity when you need it.
Trust your instincts. If something feels wrong, it probably is. You do not need proof beyond a reasonable doubt to seek help for yourself.

terms.def: Behaviors that unintentionally protect someone from the consequences of their addiction — paying their bills, lying for them, making excuses.
A pattern where you prioritize someone else's needs over your own to an unhealthy degree. Common in addiction relationships.

tasks.contacts: Al-Anon: al-anon.org • Nar-Anon: nar-anon.org • SAMHSA Helpline: 1-800-662-4357
mistakes: Explaining away clear patterns — "they are just stressed at work" becomes a loop that delays help by months or years.
Snooping through their phone or belongings to build a case — this damages trust and shifts the conversation to your behavior instead of theirs.
Telling other family members everything you have observed before talking to the person — this can feel like ambush and makes them less likely to engage.
Focusing only on the substance and missing behavioral red flags — financial problems, secrecy, and relationship withdrawal are often earlier indicators than physical symptoms.

tasks.title: How to Talk to Someone About Their Addiction
tasks.what: How to approach a conversation about substance use with someone you love — what to say, when to say it, and what to expect.
tasks.why: How you bring this up matters enormously. The wrong approach can shut down communication entirely. The right approach opens a door — even if they do not walk through it today.
steps: Choose the right moment: they should be sober, you should be calm, and you should have privacy. Never during or after a crisis.
Use I-statements instead of accusations. Say "I am worried about you because I have noticed..." rather than "You have a problem."
Be specific about what you have observed. "Last Tuesday you missed dinner and did not call" is more effective than "You are always unreliable."
Express concern, not ultimatums (unless you are prepared to follow through). "I love you and I am scared" opens more doors than "If you do not stop, I am leaving."
Prepare for denial, anger, or deflection. This is normal. Your goal is to plant a seed, not win an argument.
Have resources ready to offer if they are open: SAMHSA number, a treatment center you have researched, an Al-Anon meeting for yourself.
Know when to stop. If the conversation escalates, it is okay to say "I love you. We can talk about this again when we are both ready."

craft.title: The CRAFT Method
craft.subtitle: Community Reinforcement and Family Training — the most evidence-based approach for families
craft.intro: CRAFT was developed by Dr. Robert Meyers at the University of New Mexico. Unlike traditional interventions that rely on confrontation, CRAFT teaches you to change the dynamics around the person's substance use — making sobriety more rewarding and substance use less comfortable. It is typically taught across 6 sessions with a trained therapist, but the core principles can be applied on your own. CRAFT works because it focuses on the one person you can actually control: you. By changing how you respond to their drinking or drug use, you shift the environment in a way that nudges them toward treatment. Use CRAFT when you have tried talking and it has not worked, when they refuse to acknowledge the problem, or when you feel stuck between enabling and cutting them off. It is designed for exactly that in-between space.

steps.label: Stop enabling
steps.desc: Identify the ways you shield them from consequences — calling in sick for them, paying their bills, cleaning up after them — and gradually stop.

steps.label: Reinforce sobriety
steps.desc: When they are sober, make it rewarding — spend quality time, express affection, do enjoyable activities. When they are using, calmly withdraw those rewards. No punishment, no lectures.

steps.label: Allow natural consequences
steps.desc: If they miss work because of drinking, let them deal with the fallout. This is not cruelty — it is allowing reality to make the case for change.

steps.label: Spot treatment-ready moments
steps.desc: Watch for windows when they express regret, fear, or openness. Have a treatment option researched and ready: "I found a program. Can I tell you about it?"

steps.label: Take care of yourself
steps.desc: CRAFT is not about suffering in silence. It is strategic, compassionate action while maintaining your own health. Your wellbeing matters throughout this process.

craft.stat: Studies show 64–74% of families using CRAFT get their loved one into treatment, compared to about 30% with other approaches.
craft.footer: Consider working with a CRAFT-trained therapist. Find one at motivationandchange.com or robertjmeyersphd.com.

terms.def: Community Reinforcement and Family Training. A 6-session evidence-based program teaching families to reduce enabling, reinforce sobriety, let natural consequences occur, and identify treatment-ready moments. Success rate of 64-74% for getting loved ones into treatment.
Allowing someone to experience the real-world results of their substance use — missed work, damaged relationships, financial problems — rather than shielding them.
A structured meeting where loved ones express concern and encourage treatment. Best done with a professional interventionist. CRAFT is an alternative that does not require confrontation.

tasks.contacts: CRAFT resources: motivationandchange.com • CRAFT creator: robertjmeyersphd.com • Association of Intervention Professionals: aipro.net • Al-Anon: al-anon.org
mistakes: Leading with ultimatums instead of concern — "You need to stop" closes doors, "I am worried about you" opens them.
Choosing the wrong moment — right after a blowup or while they are using. Wait for a calm, sober window.
Expecting one conversation to lead to treatment. Seed-planting is the realistic goal. Most people need multiple conversations before they are ready.
Skipping the preparation step — having treatment options researched and ready makes it possible to act when they show openness.

script.intro: When you are ready to talk to them:
lines: "I have noticed some things that worry me, and I want to talk to you about them because I care about you. Last week, [specific observation]. I am not saying this to attack you — I am saying it because I love you and I am scared."
"I have been doing some reading, and I think there might be help available. Would you be open to hearing about it? If not today, I will be here when you are ready."
script.note: You are not responsible for their reaction. You are only responsible for speaking with love and honesty.

tasks.title: Self-Care for Supporters
tasks.what: Why taking care of yourself is not selfish — it is essential. How to maintain your own mental and physical health while supporting someone through addiction.
tasks.why: Supporters burn out. Depression, anxiety, and health problems are extremely common among people living with someone else's addiction. You cannot help them if you collapse.
steps: Attend an Al-Anon or Nar-Anon meeting. These groups exist specifically for people in your situation. You do not have to share — just listen.
Start or continue your own therapy. You are processing grief, fear, anger, and loss — even if the person is still alive.
Maintain your basic needs: sleep, nutrition, exercise, and time with people who support you.

Learn the difference between enabling and supporting. Enabling: paying their rent so they avoid consequences, lying to their boss, giving them money. Supporting: driving them to a meeting, attending Al-Anon, saying "I love you and I will not watch you destroy yourself."
Set boundaries that protect your wellbeing. "I will not lend money" or "I will not engage when you are intoxicated" are not punishments — they are self-preservation.
Remember the three things you cannot do: you did not CAUSE it, you cannot CONTROL it, and you cannot CURE it.
Stop keeping the secret if it is destroying you. You deserve to talk about what you are going through.
Recognize the signs of codependency: putting their needs above your own health, making excuses for their behavior, feeling responsible for their choices.
Set financial boundaries — stop giving money, protect shared accounts, monitor joint credit.
Give yourself permission to grieve — for the relationship you expected, for the time lost, for the fear you live with every day. This is called ambiguous loss, and it is real.

terms.def: A mutual support program specifically for friends and family of people with alcohol problems. Based on 12-step principles.
Like Al-Anon but for families of people with drug addiction.
A pattern where you prioritize someone else's needs over your own to an unhealthy degree. Common in addiction relationships.

tasks.contacts: Al-Anon: al-anon.org • Nar-Anon: nar-anon.org • Psychology Today therapist finder: psychologytoday.com/us/therapists • Crisis Text Line: text HOME to 741741
mistakes: Canceling your own therapy or doctor appointments because "they need you more" — you cannot sustain this. Burnout leads to resentment, health problems, and worse decision-making.
Monitoring their every move as your primary coping mechanism — this is hypervigilance, not support. It keeps your nervous system in constant fight-or-flight.
Only talking to people who tell you what you want to hear — Al-Anon and therapy offer honest perspective, not just comfort.
Setting boundaries and then immediately abandoning them — inconsistent boundaries teach the person that your limits are negotiable.

tasks.title: Treatment Options and How to Choose
tasks.what: An overview of treatment types — detox, inpatient, outpatient, PHP, IOP, MAT, and sober living — so you understand what options exist and how to evaluate them.
tasks.why: Treatment is not one-size-fits-all. Understanding the options helps you have informed conversations and support the right level of care.
steps: Understand the levels of care: Detox (medical withdrawal supervision) → Inpatient/Residential (24/7 facility, 28-90 days) → PHP (day treatment) → IOP (9-19 hours/week, live at home) → Outpatient (therapy sessions while living independently) → Sober Living (structured group home).
If they need detox first, confirm the program offers medical detox or can refer. Alcohol and benzodiazepine withdrawal can be life-threatening.
Ask about dual diagnosis treatment. If they have co-occurring mental health conditions (depression, anxiety, PTSD), both conditions must be treated simultaneously.
Check accreditation. Look for CARF (Commission on Accreditation of Rehabilitation Facilities) or Joint Commission accreditation.
Verify insurance coverage. Call the number on the back of the insurance card and ask about substance use treatment benefits, including in-network providers and pre-authorization requirements.
Call 3-5 treatment centers and ask: What is your program model? What is the staff-to-patient ratio? Do you offer family programming? What does aftercare look like?
If cost is a barrier, explore state-funded programs, sliding scale fees, treatment scholarships, and SAMHSA's treatment locator at findtreatment.gov.
Prepare a go-bag with essential documents (ID, insurance card, medication list) in case they agree to treatment suddenly.

terms.def: Medical supervision of withdrawal from a substance. Can be inpatient or outpatient. Required before rehab for alcohol, benzodiazepines, and opioids.
Medication-Assisted Treatment. FDA-approved medications combined with therapy. Includes naltrexone, buprenorphine, and methadone.
Treatment where the person lives at the facility 24/7. Typically 28, 60, or 90 days.
Intensive Outpatient Program. Structured treatment (9-19 hours/week) that allows the person to live at home.
Partial Hospitalization Program. Day treatment — structured program during the day, home at night. More intensive than IOP.
A structured group home for people in early recovery. Rules, accountability, peer support. Bridge between rehab and full independence.
Commission on Accreditation of Rehabilitation Facilities. Gold standard accreditation for treatment programs.

tasks.contacts: SAMHSA Treatment Locator: findtreatment.gov • Insurance company (number on back of card) • CARF directory: carf.org • Joint Commission: qualitycheck.org
mistakes: Choosing the first facility that calls you back — many treatment centers use aggressive marketing. Always verify CARF or Joint Commission accreditation before committing.
Signing financial agreements without reading them — some programs charge $30,000+ and do not accept insurance. Get costs in writing and verify coverage first.
Sending someone to a program that does not treat their co-occurring conditions — if they have depression, PTSD, or anxiety, the program must address both. Ask specifically about dual diagnosis.
Skipping the aftercare question during intake — ask "What happens after the 28 days?" If the answer is vague, that is a red flag. Good programs have structured aftercare plans.

tasks.title: Health Complications and Comorbidities
tasks.what: Understanding the medical complications that can arise from substance use — so you know what to watch for and when to seek emergency care.
tasks.why: Addiction is not just a behavioral issue — it causes serious physical harm. Knowing the risks helps you recognize medical emergencies and advocate for proper care.
steps: Learn the signs of alcohol-related emergencies: confusion, vomiting while unconscious, seizures, slow breathing, blue-tinged skin. Call 911 immediately.
Understand pancreatitis — inflammation of the pancreas, commonly caused by heavy drinking. Symptoms include severe abdominal pain, nausea, fever. Life-threatening if drinking continues.
Know the signs of opioid overdose: pinpoint pupils, slow or stopped breathing, blue lips, unresponsiveness. Naloxone (Narcan) can reverse it — many pharmacies carry it without prescription.
Recognize liver disease symptoms: yellowing skin or eyes (jaundice), abdominal swelling, confusion, easy bruising.
Understand that mental health conditions often co-occur: depression, anxiety, PTSD, bipolar disorder. These must be treated alongside addiction.
Ask their medical team about all conditions being treated. As a supporter, you may not have HIPAA access — but you can always give information to providers even if they cannot share back.

terms.def: Inflammation of the pancreas. Often caused by heavy alcohol use. Can be acute or chronic. Life-threatening if drinking continues.
Medication that rapidly reverses opioid overdose. Available at many pharmacies without prescription.
Severe alcohol withdrawal involving confusion, rapid heartbeat, fever, and seizures. Can be fatal without medical treatment.

tasks.contacts: 911 for emergencies • Poison Control: 1-800-222-1222 • SAMHSA Helpline: 1-800-662-4357 • Local pharmacy for naloxone
mistakes: Letting someone "sleep it off" when they are unresponsive — alcohol poisoning and opioid overdose can be fatal. If you cannot wake them, call 911.
Not having naloxone (Narcan) in the house when opioids are involved — it is available without prescription at most pharmacies and can be the difference between life and death.
Dismissing jaundice (yellow skin or eyes) as "they just look tired" — this is a sign of liver failure and requires immediate medical attention.

Treating mental health and addiction as separate problems — depression, PTSD, and anxiety drive substance use. Treating only the addiction without addressing the underlying condition leads to relapse.

tasks.title: Legal and Financial Considerations
tasks.what: How addiction intersects with legal issues, financial protection, and family law — from DUI consequences to involuntary commitment options.
tasks.why: Addiction creates legal and financial chaos. Understanding the landscape helps you protect yourself and make informed decisions about what to do next.
steps: Separate your finances if they are shared. Open individual bank accounts and monitor joint credit cards for unusual activity.

Check your state's involuntary commitment laws (sometimes called Baker Act, Marchman Act, or Casey's Law depending on the state). These allow families to petition for court-ordered treatment in some circumstances.
If there are children in the home, understand that substance use can be a factor in custody determinations. Document concerns and consult a family law attorney.
If they have been arrested (DUI, possession, etc.), understand that drug courts and diversion programs exist in many jurisdictions as alternatives to traditional prosecution.
Review insurance policies — health, auto, homeowners — to understand what is covered and what might be at risk.
Consult an attorney if you are considering separation, divorce, or need to protect shared assets. Many offer free initial consultations.
Do not co-sign loans, bail them out of jail, or pay their legal fees unless you have made a deliberate decision to do so — and understand the financial risk.

terms.def: Florida law allowing families to petition for involuntary substance use assessment and treatment. Similar laws exist in other states under different names.
Specialized court program offering treatment and monitoring as an alternative to incarceration for substance-related offenses.
Pre-trial program that diverts eligible defendants from traditional prosecution into treatment or community service.

tasks.contacts: Local family law attorney • Legal Aid: lawhelp.org • Your state court system (drug court information) • SAMHSA Helpline: 1-800-662-4357
mistakes: Bailing them out of jail reflexively — an arrest can be the natural consequence that motivates change. Consult a lawyer before posting bail.
Keeping joint credit cards active "just in case" — addiction-driven spending can destroy your credit score and drain shared accounts in days.
Not knowing your state's involuntary commitment laws — in many states, families can petition for court-ordered assessment. This option exists even when the person refuses help.
Hiring a criminal defense attorney without exploring drug court — diversion programs often lead to treatment and have better long-term outcomes than traditional prosecution.

tasks.title: Recovery, Relapse, and the Long Road
tasks.what: What to expect after treatment ends — including relapse statistics, warning signs, and how to support long-term recovery without becoming the sobriety police.
tasks.why: Treatment is the beginning of recovery, not the end. Relapse occurs in 40-60% of cases — comparable to relapse rates for diabetes and hypertension. Being prepared helps everyone.
steps: Discuss the aftercare plan with the treatment team before discharge. This should include therapy schedule, support group attendance, medication management, and sober living if appropriate.
Prepare the home environment: remove alcohol and substances, establish routines, discuss expectations for the first weeks.
Learn the warning signs of relapse: isolation, skipping meetings, mood changes, reconnecting with old using friends, overconfidence about sobriety.
Build a relapse response plan together. If they use again: who do they call? What do you do? Having a plan prevents panic.
If relapse happens, it is not failure — it is a setback that requires re-engagement with treatment. Do not treat it as a reason to give up.
Continue your own support work: Al-Anon, therapy, self-care. Your recovery from this experience matters too.
Have honest conversations about rebuilding trust. This takes time — months or years, not weeks.
Celebrate milestones, but do not make sobriety the center of every conversation. They need to build an identity beyond being in recovery.

terms.def: Returning to substance use after a period of sobriety. Occurs in 40-60% of recovery cases. Not a failure — a setback requiring re-engagement with treatment.
The ongoing treatment plan after completing a program — therapy, meetings, medication, and lifestyle changes.
A structured group home for people in early recovery. Rules, accountability, peer support. Bridge between rehab and independence.

tasks.contacts: Treatment center aftercare coordinator • Al-Anon: al-anon.org • SAMHSA Helpline: 1-800-662-4357 • Psychology Today therapist finder: psychologytoday.com/us/therapists
mistakes: Celebrating their 30-day chip and then acting like the problem is solved — early recovery is the most vulnerable period. The real work is the first 6-12 months.
Checking their phone, smelling their breath, or searching their room — this is hypervigilance, not support. It damages trust and puts you in a parental role that harms the relationship.
Bringing alcohol into the home or attending events where drinking is central "because they should be able to handle it" — in early recovery, environment matters enormously.
Dropping your own therapy or Al-Anon because "they are better now" — your recovery from the trauma of living with addiction is separate from their sobriety and takes its own time.

tasks.title: Resource Directory for Supporters
tasks.what: A comprehensive directory of hotlines, organizations, support groups, and tools for people supporting someone through addiction.
tasks.why: Having resources organized in one place saves time during a crisis and ensures you always know where to turn.
steps: Save the SAMHSA National Helpline: 1-800-662-4357. Free, confidential, 24/7, 365 days a year. Available in English and Spanish.
Find local Al-Anon meetings at al-anon.org/al-anon-meetings. Online meetings are also available.
Find Nar-Anon meetings for families of people with drug addiction at nar-anon.org.
Use SAMHSA's treatment locator at findtreatment.gov to search for treatment programs by location, substance, and insurance.
For opioid-specific resources, contact the NIDA helpline or visit nida.nih.gov.
For crisis situations, text HOME to 741741 (Crisis Text Line) or call 988 (Suicide and Crisis Lifeline).
For legal resources, visit lawhelp.org to find free legal aid in your state.
For insurance questions, call the number on the back of the insurance card and ask about substance use treatment benefits.
For additional support groups: SMART Recovery Family & Friends (smartrecovery.org) offers a non-12-step alternative.
For financial assistance: state-funded treatment programs, SAMHSA block grant programs, and sliding scale clinics are available in most areas.
Recommended reading: "Beyond Addiction" (Foote), "Codependent No More" (Beattie), "Beautiful Boy" (Sheff).
Online communities: r/AlAnon on Reddit, In The Rooms (intherooms.com) for virtual meetings.
tasks.contacts: SAMHSA Helpline: 1-800-662-4357 • 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline • Crisis Text Line: text HOME to 741741 • Al-Anon: al-anon.org • Nar-Anon: nar-anon.org • SMART Recovery: smartrecovery.org • FindTreatment.gov • NIDA: nida.nih.gov • Patient Advocate Foundation: patientadvocate.org
mistakes: Not saving emergency numbers before you need them.
Relying on only one resource or support channel.
Forgetting that resources exist for YOU, not just for the person with the addiction.
